content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
I 428 J Street• Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Bill Sarton 
City Attorney 
333 Civic Center Plaza 
Tracy, CA 95376 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-027 
Dear Mr. Sarton: 
April 10, 2017 
You are the City Attorney for the City of Tracy. This letter responds to your request for 
advice regarding conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act")1 and Section 
1090 on behalf of City Councilmember Rhodesia Ransom. Please note that we do not advise on any 
other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also 
not a finder of fact when rendering advice (/11 re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice 
we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and Section 1090, not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law 
conflict of interest. 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Joaquin County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097. l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section I 090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097. l(c)(S).) 
QUESTIONS 
1. May City Councilmember Ransom participate in a decision to approve the citywide budget? 
2. Does Section 1090 prohibit the City from entering into a contract with Sow a Seed if City 
Councilmember Ransom serves as the Executive Director of that 501(c)(3) nonprofit 
organization? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. A-17-027 
Page No. 2 
1. Yes. City Councilmember Ransom may participate on the final vote to adopt or reject the 
agency's budget if all separate decisions related to the budget have been finalized even if 
that budget includes the allocation for the Grant Program provided that the decision does not 
reopen or in any way alter the decisions from which she was disqualified. 
2. No. Section l 090 does not prohibit the City from entering into a contract with Sow a Seed if 
Councilmember Ransom recuses herself from the decision because a remote interest 
exception applies. 
FACTS 
City of Tracy City Councilmember Rhodesia Ransom currently serves as the Executive 
Director for Sow a Seed, a 50l(c) non-profit organization dedicated to mentoring at risk youth. 
Councilmember Ransom has received compensation from the organization for her service in excess 
of$500 over the past twelve months. Her husband currently serves on the organization's Board of 
Directors. His position is uncompensated. 
Sow a Seed currently receives, and has previously received, grant funding from the City 
through the Mayor's Community Youth Support Network ("Youth Support Network") and 
Reconnecting Our Youth Grant Program ("Grant Program"). The Youth Support Network is a 
collaborative effort involving City, County, and State entities, community-based organizations, 
businesses, law enforcement officials, community service providers and community members 
committed to reaching out and reconnecting youth and families to services. The Grant Program, 
established in 2008 by the City Council, seeks to support local programs that develop skills and 
competencies resulting in healthy and thriving youth and families. The Grant Program is 
administered by the City Manager's Office. The City solicits applications from nonprofit 
organizations and other governmental agencies through a Request for Qualifications (RFQ) process. 
A review team comprised of volunteers, who are selected by staff based on their subject matter 
expertise and/or experience in the field of youth and child development and community service, 
selects grantees based on their review of the applications. The application process is competitive 
and not all of the organizations that apply receive a grant. Selected organizations have received 
grants up to $50,000. Recipients may only use up to 40% of the grant funds to cover personnel 
costs. 
Since 2008 the City Council has approved funding for the Grant Program during the annual 
budget approval process as a line item in the departmental budget for the City Manager's Office. 
However, continued City support for the program is not guaranteed. Organizations selected to 
receive a grant enter into a one-year funding agreement with the City that is signed by the City 
Manager. 
Sow a Seed received a grant from the City for fiscal year 2016-2017 in the amount of 
$50,000. Sow a Seed has been selected as a grant recipient for the last four years. City Council 
Member Ransom has informed your office that Sow a Seed's allocation of Grant Program funding 
for her position has ranged from $0 to $6,000 during the last four grant cycles. Sow a Seed also 


File No. A-17-027 
Page No. 3 
receives funding from private parties, other public agencies, and private foundations. The 
organization's annual operating budget is approximately $190,000, and it currently employs seven 
individuals, in addition to non-paid interns. 
ANALYSIS 
The Political Reform Act 
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, 
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. An official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on the official, or on a member of the official's immediate family, or on the 
following financial interests pertinent to your facts: 
• Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by a commercial lending 
institution in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard 
to official status, aggregating $500 or more in value provided to, received by or promised to 
the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 
87103(c).) 
• Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
While Sow a Seed is not a "business entity" as defined in the Act2, nonprofit corporations 
such as Sow a Seed can be considered sources of income under Section 87103(c). You stated that 
City Councilmember Ransom is the Executive Director for Sow a Seed and received $500 or more 
in compensation in a 12-month period from Sow a Seed. Thus, she may not make, participate in 
making, or in any way use her official position to influence any decision that will have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on Sow a Seed. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Generally, a governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's source of 
income is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject 
of, the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) In all other cases (such as this one), the financial effect is 
foreseeable if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701 (b ). ) If the financial 
effect cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's 
control, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (Ibid.) 
Under Regulation 18702.3(a)(3), a financial effect on a nonprofit entity that is a source of 
income is material if: 
l Section 8'.!005 defines a "business entity" as any organization or enterprise operated for profit, including but 
not limited lo a proprietorship, partnership, finn, business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or association. 


File No. A-17-027 
Page No. 4 
"(3) The source is a nonprofit that will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss." 
Under your facts, should the city's budget be approved, Sow a Seed would likely receive a 
measurable benefit as Sow a Seed has been a recipient of funds from the Grant Program for several 
years. Most recently, Sow a Seed received a grant of $50,000, which amounts to approximately 
25% of its entire operating budget of $190,000. Therefore, there would be a reasonably foreseeable 
material financial effect upon City Councilmember Ransom's economic interest as a result of the 
governmental decision to approve the City's budget and she would have a conflict of interest in 
participating in that decision.3 
Segmentation 
Under certain circumstances, a public official disqualified from one decision may participate 
in other related decisions provided that the official's participation does not affect the decision in 
which he or she has a conflict of interest. (/11 re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77). 
Commission staff has consistently advised that an official may segment a decision in which 
the official has a conflict of interest from other decisions in which he or she does not have a conflict 
of interest to allow participation by the official in one or several related decisions as long as the 
decisions are not too interrelated to be considered separately. 
The Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343 outlined a procedure to be followed when 
segmenting one decision from another. The Commission codified its advice, providing the 
conditions under which governmental decisions may be segmented and the method to follow to 
allow participation by officials who would otherwise have a conflict of interest. 
Regulation 18706 provides the rules for such "segmentation" of a governmental decision: 
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a financial 
interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the following 
conditions apply: 
"(I) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken 
down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision 
in which the official has a disqualifying financial interest; 
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from 
the other decisions; 
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first 
3 When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (such as a city councilmcmber) has 
a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (I) immediately prior to the 
discussion of the item, orally identify each type of interest involved in the decision as well as details of the interest on 
the record of the meeting, (2) recuse himself or herself, and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or 
vote on the item. (Section 87105; Regulation 18707.) 


File No. A-17-027 
Page No. 5 
and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's 
participation in any way; and 
"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been 
made, the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a 
reopening of, or otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official 
was disqualified. 
"(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are "inextricably interrelated" 
when the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter 
the result of another decision." 
"(c) Budget Decisions and General Plan Adoption or Amendment Decisions 
Affecting an Entire Jurisdiction: Once all the separate decisions related to 
a budget or general plan affecting the entire jurisdiction have been finalized, the 
public official may participate in the final vote to adopt or reject the 
agency's budget or to adopt, reject, or amend the general plan." 
Under the segmentation method, City Councilmember Ransom may not participate in the 
approval of the entire budget until the decision in which she has a conflict of interest is decided, 
with her leaving the room and not participating in any discussion or decision affecting the decision 
for which she has a conflict of interest. Under Regulation 18706(a)(3), the decision in which an 
official has a financial interest must be considered first. 
Additionally, under Regulation 18706(c) Councilmember Ransom may participate on the 
final vote to adopt or reject the agency's budget if all separate decisions related to the budget have 
been finalized even if that budget includes the allocation for the Grant Program provided that 
the decision does not reopen or in any way alter the decisions from which she was disqualified. 
(Cook Advice Letter, No .. A-83-163.) 
Section 1090 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section I 090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Under Section 1090, "the prohibited 
act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. 
Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void, regardless of 
whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 
Cal.3d 633, 646-649.) When Section 1090 is applicable to one member of a governing body of a 
public entity, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member abstain; the 
entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. (Id. at pp. 647-649.) 


File No. A-17-027 
Page No. 6 
We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether a public officer has a disqualifying 
conflict of interest under Section 1090. Steps one through four are not at issue here: (1) City 
Councilmember Ransom is subject to Section I 090 because she is a city councilmember; (2) Grants 
are generally "contracts" under Section l 090,4 and therefore, a grant awarded to Sow a Seed would 
be a contract; (3) An officer is conclusively presumed to be involved in the making of his or her 
agency's contracts when the officer is a member of a board or commission that has the power to 
execute the contract at issue. (Thomson, supra, p. 649.) Therefore, City Councilmember Ransom 
would make or participate in the making of a contract if she participated in the contract with Sow a 
Seed; and (4) City Councilmember Ransom would have a financial interest in the contract because 
she is the Executive Director of Sow a Seed and the grant money has the potential to be used to pay 
part of her salary as noted above. 
Therefore, Section 1090 prohibits City Councilmember Ransom from making or 
participating in the contract decision to Sow a Seed and would prohibit the City Council from 
entering into the contract unless an exception applies. 
Step Five: Does either a remote or noninterest exception apply? 
The Legislature has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition where 
the financial interest involved is deemed a "remote interest," as defined in Section 1091, or a 
"noninterest," as defined in Section 1091.5. 
If a "remote interest" is present, the contract may be made if: ( 1) the officer discloses his or 
her financial interest in the contract to the public agency; (2) the interest is noted in the public 
agency's official records; and (3) the officer abstains from any participation in the making of the 
contract. (Section 1091 (a); Brunner Advice Letter, No. A-16-168; 88 Ops.CalAtty.Gen. 106, I 08 
(2005).) If a "noninterest" is present, the contract may be made without the officer's abstention. 
(City of Vernon v. Central Basin Mun. Water Dist. (1999) 69.Cal.App4th 508, 514-515; 84 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 159-160 (2001 ).) 
Section 1091 (b )( 1) sets forth a remote interest exception and provides in pertinent part as 
follows: 
"(b) As used in this article, "remote interest" means any of the following . . .  : 
"(I) That of an officer or employee of a nonprofit entity exempt from taxation 
pursuant to Section 50l(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code (26 U.S.C. Sec. 
50l(c)(3)), pursuant to Section 501(c){5) of the Internal Revenue Code (26 U.S.C. 
Sec. 501(c)(5)), or a nonprofit corporation." 
City Councilmember Ransom is the Executive Director of Sow a Seed, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit 
entity. Therefore, we find that she has a remote interest in the contract with Sow a Seed. Because 
she has a remote interest in the contract, the City may enter into a contract with Sow a Seed only if: 
(1) City Councilmember Ransom discloses her financial interest in the contract to the City; 
4 People 1•. Honig, supra. 48 Cal.App.4th 289. The court rejected a claim that a grant was not a contract within 
the meaning of Section 1090. 


File No. A-17-027 
Page No. 7 
(2) her interest is noted in the City's official records; and (3) she abstains from any participation in 
the making of the contract. (Section 1091(a); Bru1111er Advice Letter, No. A-16-168; 88 
Ops.CalAtty.Gen. 106, 108 (2005).) 
Step Six: Does the rule of necessity apply? 
There are no facts suggesting that the .. rule of necessity" would apply in the present 
situation. Therefore, this Section I 090 analysis ends here. 
Thus, we conclude that Section 1090 prohibits City Councilmember Ransom from making 
or participating in the making of a contract with Sow a Seed. However, the City may enter into a 
contract with Sow a Seed pursuant to the remote interest exception set forth in Section 1091{b)(l) 
so long as ( 1) City Councilmember Ransom discloses her financial interest in the contract to the 
City, (2) her interest is noted in the City's official records, and (3) she abstains from any 
participation in the making of the contract. 
We also conclude that the Act prohibits City Councilmember Ransom from taking part in 
decisions relating to the contract with Sow a Seed. The Act requires her to recuse herself from that 
decision and prohibits her from making any contacts or appearances before a City official or 
employee regarding the contract. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
SKB:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
·{& 
By: 
Sukhi K. Brar 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's

Section 1091 (b )( 1) sets forth a remote interest exception and provides in pertinent part as 
follows:

pursuant to Section 50l(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code (26 U.S.C. Sec. 
50l(c)(3)), pursuant to Section 501(c){5) of the Internal Revenue Code (26 U.S.C. 
Sec. 501(c)(5)), or a nonprofit corporation."

entity. Therefore, we find that she has a remote interest in the contract with Sow a Seed. Because 
she has a remote interest in the contract, the City may enter into a contract with Sow a Seed only if: 
(1) City Councilmember Ransom discloses her financial interest in the contract to the City; 
4 People 1•. Honig, supra. 48 Cal.App.4th 289. The court rejected a claim that a grant was not a contract within 
the meaning of Section 1090.  
(2) her interest is noted in the City's official records; and (3) she abstains from any participation in 
the making of the contract. (Section 1091(a); Bru1111er Advice Letter, No. A-16-168; 88 
Ops.CalAtty.Gen. 106, 108 (2005).) 
Step Six: Does the rule of necessity apply? 
There are no facts suggesting that the .. rule of necessity" would apply in the present 
situation. Therefore, this Section I 090 analysis ends here. 
Thus, we conclude that Section 1090 prohibits City Councilmember Ransom from making 
or participating in the making of a contract with Sow a Seed. However, the City may enter into a 
contract with Sow a Seed pursuant to the remote interest exception set forth in Section 1091{b)(l) 
so long as ( 1) City Councilmember Ransom discloses her financial interest in the contract to the 
City, (2) her interest is noted in the City's official records, and (3) she abstains from any 
participation in the making of the contract. 
We also conclude that the Act prohibits City Councilmember Ransom from taking part in 
decisions relating to the contract with Sow a Seed. The Act requires her to recuse herself from that 
decision and prohibits her from making any contacts or appearances before a City official or 
employee regarding the contract.

QUESTION: 1. May City Councilmember Ransom participate in a decision to approve the citywide budget? 
2. Does Section 1090 prohibit the City from entering into a contract with Sow a Seed if City 
Councilmember Ransom serves as the Executive Director of that 501(c)(3) nonprofit 
organization? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in

CONCLUSION: 1. Yes. City Councilmember Ransom may participate on the final vote to adopt or reject the 
agency's budget if all separate decisions related to the budget have been finalized even if 
that budget includes the allocation for the Grant Program provided that the decision does not 
reopen or in any way alter the decisions from which she was disqualified. 
2. No. Section l 090 does not prohibit the City from entering into a contract with Sow a Seed if 
Councilmember Ransom recuses herself from the decision because a remote interest 
exception applies.

Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (/11 re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and Section 1090, not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest. In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Joaquin County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section I 090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. CONCLUSIONS File No. A-17-027 Page No. 2 1. Yes. City Councilmember Ransom may participate on the final vote to adopt or reject the agency's budget if all separate decisions related to the budget have been finalized even if that budget includes the allocation for the Grant Program provided that the decision does not reopen or in any way alter the decisions from which she was disqualified. 2. No. Section l 090 does not prohibit the City from entering into a contract with Sow a Seed if Councilmember Ransom recuses herself from the decision because a remote interest exception applies.
